judgments.comparative_verdict: Both reports hit all four expectations with solid commit citations; Report 2 is marginally more precise (explicitly names /reports/monthly in the failure narrative and states the team 'returned to' Cargobox), but the two are comparable in substance.

report_1.evidence: git log in carelog-api — the single most important check: it shows this migration was already done and reverted (commits 454a21f, 597777b, 1712565, all PLAT-31).

report_2.evidence: commits `454a21f` (Edgerunner deploy), `597777b` (per-request Postgres connections stopgap), and `1712565` (revert back to Cargobox), all under ticket PLAT-31, April 2026.

report_1.evidence: Commit 1712565 (2026-04-21) records that on Edgerunner, per-request Postgres connections "opened faster than Postgres could recycle them and tripped the connection limit ('FATAL: too many connections')", taking the endpoint down" under normal dashboard load.

report_2.evidence: Revert commit `1712565` records the outcome: under normal dashboard load, per-request connections opened faster than Postgres recycled them, `/reports/monthly` exhausted `max_connections` ("FATAL: too many connections"), and the endpoint went down; the team returned to the always-on Cargobox container with the shared pool.

report_1.evidence: This is a re-attempt of PLAT-31, which caused a production outage less than three months ago (April 2026), and nothing in the repo has changed to address the root cause.

report_2.evidence: the team returned to the always-on Cargobox container with the shared pool.

report_1.evidence: People to involve: whoever owned PLAT-31 and the April incident (they have the context the commits summarize)... and leadership — who should be shown the revert commit before reaffirming the decision.

report_2.evidence: bring the PLAT-31 history to the platform/system owner (whoever owns carelog-api and the PLAT ticket queue) and to whoever owns the Postgres instance... The fact that this was tried and reverted is itself information leadership needs before committing spend to a second attempt.
